metadata
  document-css: false
  link-citations: true
  date-format: long
  lang: en
  theme:
    - lux
    - custom.scss
  title: Modeling Daycare Accessibility in Mecklenburg County, NC Using `accessibility` and `r5r`
  subtitle: A Reproducible Documentation
  author:
    - name: Angel Rutherford
      affiliations:
        - name: University of Pennslyvania
  date: today
  abstract: Potochnick et al. (2024) identified persistent disparities in early care and education (ECE) access among Black and Latino families in Mecklenburg County, North Carolina, finding that geographic proximity alone could not account for why these families underutilize subsidized childcare. Their landscape assessment mapped the distribution of prenatal through age three services but noted that a surface-level spatial analysis obscures the more nuanced access barriers families face in practice. This study builds directly on that foundation, arguing that a robust measure of effective access must move beyond facility location to account for the factors that shape whether a facility is a realistic option for a given family, including licensed capacity relative to child population, quality as measured by North Carolina's star-rated licensing system, subsidy acceptance, and the distance based on mode of transportation available to a household. Using a Two-Step Floating Catchment Area (2SFCA) model applied at the census block group level, this analysis constructs a series of accessibility scores that isolate these dimensions of effective access and examines their spatial distribution alongside sociodemographic vulnerability indicators outlined in the original work.
  bibliography:
    - references.bib
  reference-section-title: References
  toc-location: right
  toc-title: Contents
  smooth-scroll: true

  abstract: Potochnick et al. (2024) identified persistent disparities in early care and education (ECE) access among Black and Latino families in Mecklenburg County, North Carolina, finding that geographic proximity alone could not account for why these families underutilize subsidized childcare. Their landscape assessment mapped the distribution of prenatal through age three services but noted that a surface-level spatial analysis obscures the more nuanced access barriers families face in practice. This study builds directly on that foundation, arguing that a robust measure of effective access must move beyond facility location to account for the factors that shape whether a facility is a realistic option for a given family, including licensed capacity relative to child population, quality as measured by North Carolina's star-rated licensing system, subsidy acceptance, and the distance based on mode of transportation available to a household. Using a Two-Step Floating Catchment Area (2SFCA) model applied at the census block group level, this analysis constructs a series of accessibility scores that isolate these dimensions of effective access and examines their spatial distribution alongside sociodemographic vulnerability indicators outlined in the original work.

Using a Two-Step Floating Catchment Area (2SFCA) model applied at the census block group level, this analysis constructs a series of accessibility scores that isolate these dimensions of effective access and examines their spatial distribution alongside sociodemographic vulnerability indicators outlined in the original work.
  bibliography:
    - references.bib
  reference-section-title: References
  toc-location: right
  toc-title: Contents
  smooth-scroll: true

    - custom.scss
  title: Modeling Daycare Accessibility in Mecklenburg County, NC Using `accessibility` and `r5r`
  subtitle: A Reproducible Documentation
  author:
    - name: Angel Rutherford
      affiliations:
        - name: University of Pennslyvania
  date: today
  abstract: Potochnick et al. (2024) identified persistent disparities in early care and education (ECE) access among Black and Latino families in Mecklenburg County, North Carolina, finding that geographic proximity alone could not account for why these families underutilize subsidized childcare. Their landscape assessment mapped the distribution of prenatal through age three services but noted that a surface-level spatial analysis obscures the more nuanced access barriers families face in practice. This study builds directly on that foundation, arguing that a robust measure of effective access must move beyond facility location to account for the factors that shape whether a facility is a realistic option for a given family, including licensed capacity relative to child population, quality as measured by North Carolina's star-rated licensing system, subsidy acceptance, and the distance based on mode of transportation available to a household. Using a Two-Step Floating Catchment Area (2SFCA) model applied at the census block group level, this analysis constructs a series of accessibility scores that isolate these dimensions of effective access and examines their spatial distribution alongside sociodemographic vulnerability indicators outlined in the original work.
  bibliography:
    - references.bib
  reference-section-title: References
  toc-location: right
  toc-title: Contents
  smooth-scroll: true
